metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

page_content: Dukkha (not being at ease, 'suffering') is an innate characteristic of transient existence;

metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

page_content: nothing is forever, this is painful;

metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

page_content: Samudaya (origin, cause): together with this transient world and its pain, there is also thirst (desire, longing, craving) for and attachment to this transient, unsatisfactory existence;

metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

page_content: Nirodha (cessation, ending, confinement): the attachment to this transient world and its pain can be severed or contained by the confinement or letting go of this craving;

metadata.source: Source document: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, Four noble truth, english translation by Wikipedia
metadata.author: Authors of Wikipedia

page_content: Marga (road, path, way): the Noble Eightfold Path is the path leading to the confinement of this desire and attachment, and the release from dukkha.
